did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for the classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) in Harmonically Generated Microscopy (HGM) images. The method involves extracting features using Gabor filters to initialize the CNN kernels and training the network to distinguish between normal and BCC collagen fibers in SHG images, achieving an accuracy of 0.973437.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a computer-aided diagnosis method using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) initialized with Gabor filters for classifying Basal Cell Carcinoma in Harmonically Generated Microscopy images. It includes a 'Proposed Method' section detailing the algorithm, an 'Experimental Result' section with specific dataset details (295 THG images), implementation parameters (Caffe, SGD, learning rate 0.01, momentum 0.9), and quantitative results (accuracy 0.973). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE AICAS 2022), it reports new technical results and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Convolutional Neural Network Classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma in Harmonically Generated Microscopy Images
journal: 2022 IEEE 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Circuits and Systems (AICAS)
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-aided diagnosis method for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) using Harmonically Generated Microscopy (HGM) images to address the limitations of invasive biopsies. The authors utilized a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) initialized with Gabor filters to extract scale and directionality features from Third Harmonic Generation (THG) images of dendritic melanocytes. The dataset consisted of 295 THG images with a 512x512 resolution, which were split into 80% training and 20% testing sets. The CNN architecture included two convolutional layers with Gabor-initialized kernels, two pooling layers, and two fully-connected layers, implemented using the Caffe framework with Stochastic Gradient Descent. The model achieved a classification accuracy of 0.973437, demonstrating fast convergence and high efficiency in distinguishing between normal and BCC collagen fibers.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the classification task. The dataset consisted of 295 THG images from BCC patients, specifically focusing on infected dendritic melanocytes and collagen fibers in the papillary dermis.
The comparator class used in the binary classification task. The dataset included normal skin samples (healthy human) to distinguish from BCC samples. Images were annotated by dermatologists.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of collagen fibers in SHG images as either normal or Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a computer-aided diagnosis method using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify collagen fibers in Harmonically Generated Microscopy (HGM) images. The task is binary classification distinguishing between 'normal' and 'BCC' (Basal Cell Carcinoma) samples. The model uses Gabor filter-initialized kernels and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with softmax loss to predict discrete diagnostic categories. The reported metric is classification accuracy (0.973437).

datasets_used.name: HHGM/THG Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of Third Harmonic Generation (THG) images obtained from Harmonically Generated Microscopy (HHGM) virtual biopsy of the stratum basale of human skin. The images were annotated by dermatologists and focused on dendritic melanocytes and collagen fibers in the papillary dermis.

use.training: 80% of the total dataset was used for training the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).
use.testing: 20% of the total dataset was used for testing the model's accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance of the CNN model on the testing dataset.
To calculate the difference between prediction results and labeled information during training.
Used as a regularization term in the cost function to prevent overfitting.
Used for weight updates during the training process to minimize the loss function.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy (0.973437) and loss convergence as performance metrics.

HHGM/THG Image Dataset.notes: The dataset consisted of 295 THG images (512x512 resolution) from BCC patients and normal skin, annotated by dermatologists. The data was split into 80% training and 20% testing sets. The model was trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 64, learning rate of 0.01, and momentum of 0.9. The loss converged after 10 epochs. The final accuracy of 0.973437 was achieved on the testing set. Table 4 in the paper compares this accuracy (0.973) with related work [12] which reported an accuracy of 0.981.

architecture_used.pretraining: None; kernels initialized with handcrafted Gabor filters
architecture_used.input_size: 256x256 (derived from 512x512 images split into four sub-images)

architecture_details.down_sampling: Averaging (to reduce 512x512 to 256x256, then further processing implied by framework)

preprocessing_techniques: Gabor filter feature extraction for scale and directionality information
Splitting 512x512 images into four 256x256 sub-images to reduce data dimension
conclusions: The proposed computer-aided diagnosis method using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) initialized with Gabor filters can accurately classify Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) in Harmonically Generated Microscopy (HGM) images, achieving a classification accuracy of 0.973437.

Initializing CNN kernels with handcrafted Gabor filters allows for automatic adjustment of scale range and more accurate segmentation of infected basal cells, resulting in faster convergence (loss converged after 10 epochs) and efficient feature extraction.
The method provides a non-invasive alternative to traditional pathological diagnosis, avoiding the time, labor, and risks associated with invasive biopsies and staining procedures.
The proposed algorithm effectively reduces time and labor consumption by automating the analysis of large amounts of HGM data, offering significant potential for computer-aided skin cancer diagnosis.

Although the imaging quality of non-invasive HGM is generally poorer than that of invasive staining microscope images used in related works, the proposed method sustains high detection accuracy comparable to those invasive methods.

Incorporating pathological morphology (via Gabor filters) into the CNN training framework systematically integrates human expertise, leading to faster convergence compared to methods that require training separate networks or manual analysis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-center dataset of only 295 THG images, which limits statistical power and generalizability.

There is no external validation; the model was trained and tested on data from the same institution (National Cheng Kung University/National Taiwan University Hospital), raising concerns about population or dataset bias.

The paper does not report standard deviation, confidence intervals, or p-values for the reported accuracy of 0.973437, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance or robustness of the results.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods (specifically reference [12] with 0.981 accuracy) is limited by the use of invasive staining microscope images for the comparator, whereas the proposed method uses non-invasive HHGM images which inherently have lower image quality.
The data splitting strategy (80% training, 20% testing) is not described as using cross-validation, which may lead to high variance in performance estimates given the small sample size.

The paper acknowledges that the imaging quality of non-invasive HGM is generally poorer than invasive staining microscope images, yet claims comparable accuracy, which may be optimistic without rigorous statistical testing.
No data leakage risks are explicitly addressed, but the small dataset size and lack of stratified splitting details warrant caution regarding overfitting.
